content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
February 14,
1995
Jack Leguria,
Executive Director
Central California Citizens Against
Lawsuit Abuse
p. o.
Box 1913
Modesto,
CA
95353
Re:
Your Request for Informal
Assistance
Our File No.
1-95—029
Dear Mr. Leguria:
You have requested advice concerning the campaign provisions
of the Political Reform Act
(the uACt).1/
Because your questions
are general in nature,
we will treat your letter as a request for
informal assistance pursuant to Regulation 18329(c) •2/
In addition, please be advised that the Commission’s
jurisdiction is limited to the provisions of the Act.
The
Commission cannot provide advice concerning your organization’s
tax status or activities related to federal candidates or
committees.
If you have questions concerning tax or federal
election issues,
you should contact the Internal Revenue Service
and the Federal Election Commission.
1/
Government Code Sections 81000—91015.
All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et g.
All references to regulations are to Title
2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2/
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c) (3).)
428 J Street, Suite 800
•
P.O. Box 807
•
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807
•
(916) 322-5660


Jack Leguria
Our File No.
1—95—029
Page
2
QUESTIONS
1.
May Central California Citizens Against Lawsuit Abuse
(“CALA”) accept donations from political action committees?
3
!
2.
May political action committees make donations to
CALA?
CONCLUSIONS
1.
Nothing in the Act would prohibit
CALA from accepting
donations from committees.
If donations are collected or used for
campaign or lobbying purposes,
CALA may incur filing obligations
under the Act.
2.
Committees may make donations to bona fide charitable,
educational, civic, religious,
or similar tax—exempt, non—profit
organizations if the donation is reasonably related to a
political, legislative or governiuentalpurpose.
No substantial
part of the proceeds may have a material financial effect on the
committee treasurer or any individual with authority to approve
the committee’s expenditures.
ANALYSIS
The Act contains various provisions which regulate the
activities of organizations that raise or spend funds in
connection with state and local elections,
and organizations that
make payments to influence matters before the State Legislature
and state administrative agencies (i.e.,
lobbying).
The bylaws
included with your letter state that CALA’s purpose is to “promote
the mutual benefit of its supporters by educating consumers on the
negative effects of lawsuit abuse.”
However, there is nothing in
your letter or the bylaws which specifically describes the
activities in which
CALA will engage.
Therefore,
we can only
provide you with the following general information.
The Act does not prohibit
CALA from accepting donations from
committees.
If you are concerned that acceptance of donations
from committees will affect CALA’s status as a non—profit
corporation,
you should contact the Internal Revenue Service and
the California Franchise Tax Board.
3/
.
.
.
.
.
The Act does not contain a definition of “political action
committee.”
We will assume for purposes of this letter that you
are referring to organizations which receive contributions for the
purpose of supporting or opposing various California state and
local candidates and issues.
Such organizations are defined in
the Act as general purpose recipient committees.
(Sections 82013
and 82027.5.)


Jack Leguria
Our File No.
1-95-029
Page
3
Sections 89510 through 89522 require that expenditures of
campaign funds held by California candidates and committees be
reasonably related to political, legislative,
or governmental
purposes.
Expenditures that confer a substantial personal benefit
must be directly related to a political, legislative, or
governmental purpose.
Section 89515 provides:
Campaign funds may be used to make donations
or loans to bona fide charitable, educational,
civic, religious, or similar tax—exempt, nonprofit
organizations, where no substantial part of the
proceeds will have a material financial effect on
the candidate, elected officer,
campaign treasurer,
or any individual or individuals with authority to
approve the expenditure of campaign funds held by a
committee,
or member of his or her immediate
family,
and where the donation or loan bears a
reasonable relation to a political, legislative,
or
governmental purpose.
If CALà intends to use funds received from others to make
contributions or expenditures in connection with state or local
elections,
or for the purpose of lobbying the State Legislature or
state administrative agencies, please contact the Commission for
information concerning the organization’s filing obligations.
If you have questions concerning this letter, please contact
me at
(916)
322—5662.
Sincerely,
C
Carla Wardlow
Division Chief
Technical Assistance Division
